notes: Route 128-era minicomputer vendor headquartered at Prime Park, Natick, MA. Subject of chapter.
Joined Prime Computer around Thanksgiving 1979 in Market Planning; covered commercial then government verticals; left for Stratus Computer after Ken Fisher’s departure in 1981.
CEO of Prime Computer who grew the company from $10 million to a major franchise over six years; resigned/was pushed out in 1981 amid board disagreements over growth pace and capital allocation.
Investor Relations VP at Prime Computer; later became Kastner’s partner at Aberdeen Group. Also referenced in cache as charles-t-casale and c-t-casale.
Ran Prime’s Market Planning group; recruited Kastner; later led launch of Prime Office; delivered Prime Office system to the White House. Brother of an ADL colleague.
Worked alongside Kastner in Market Planning; became executive assistant to sales VP Bob Clausen after Schlegel’s reassignment.
Sales VP at Prime Computer; depicted at the Lake Tahoe sales kickoff making thousand-dollar craps bets surrounded by salespeople.
District sales manager from Ohio who beat Kastner for the director’s job; reorganized territory along vertical lines; later assigned Kastner to lead the future product requirements project in 1981.
Prime’s Australian dealer; placed Prime Computer TV commercials alongside Doctor Who, an unprecedented marketing move in the minicomputer industry.
Corporate counsel at Prime Computer; explained to Kastner’s team why Prime would not sue New Jersey over a non-compliant bid submission.
Chuck Casale later became Kastner’s partner here; Prime market-planning work described as rehearsal for Aberdeen methodology.
Kastner’s employer for eight years before Prime; ADL work on fixed-price contracts contrasted with Prime’s equity upside. Schlegel was brother of an ADL colleague.
Startup that called Kastner after Ken Fisher’s departure from Prime; subject of Chapter 5.
Named peer of Prime in analyst slide decks; Prime positioned against DEC’s VAX ecosystem on responsiveness and application integration.
Named alongside Prime and DEC as a minicomputer challenger to IBM in analyst slide decks circa 1980.
Named alongside Prime, DEC, and Data General as a potential IBM challenger from below in analyst comparisons circa 1980.
The target of minicomputer vendors including Prime; post-Fisher replacement CEO expected to be an IBM executive who would ‘professionalize’ Prime’s go-to-market machine.
Washington D.C. software company that developed PROMIS under a federal LEAA grant; Prime won hardware business for 20+ PROMIS deployments; INSLAW later alleged DOJ stole its software.
Awarded INSLAW a contract to deploy PROMIS across federal prosecutor offices; later accused of stealing INSLAW’s software.
British signals intelligence HQ in Cheltenham; Kastner visited during government market tour; a Prime system was doing satellite photo analysis on the Iran-Iraq war.
Kastner was vetted by CIA (and MI6) prior to his GCHQ visit in Cheltenham.

Named by Kastner as an example of a later Aberdeen client where executives did go to jail for revenue recognition fraud, contrasted with Prime’s clean compliance record.
Named alongside Computer Associates as an Aberdeen client where executives faced legal consequences for accounting fraud, contrasted with Prime.
Mentioned as a Prime customer doing CAD/CAM work; classified as a technical/scientific customer, which removed it from Kastner’s commercial territory.
Mentioned as a Prime customer doing avionics work; classified as technical/scientific, outside Kastner’s commercial territory.
Internal group at Prime Computer that Kastner joined in 1979; led by Peter Schlegel.
